01

Descriptive Statistics & Normality

Output

Variable N Mean SD Min Max Skewness Kurtosis Shapiro-W p Section 1 % 24 55.50 12.34 33.0 86.0 +0.586 −0.097 0.929 0.093 Section 2 % 27 66.67 9.90 50.0 85.0 +0.464 −1.199 0.830 0.000 * Grand Total % 24 61.25 8.02 47.0 73.0 +0.050 −1.100 0.941 0.172 RM 27 3.30 0.54 2.0 4.0 +0.127 −0.606 0.708 0.000 * CE 27 3.37 0.56 2.0 4.0 −0.128 −0.828 0.724 0.000 * CF 27 3.37 0.63 2.0 5.0 +0.501 +0.131 0.777 0.000 * SOD 27 3.30 0.47 3.0 4.0 +0.892 −1.204 0.576 0.000 * * p ≤ .05 — Shapiro-Wilk rejects normality

Plot — Distributions with Skewness & Cut Score

Plot — Q-Q Normality Assessment

Skewness Ranking — Most to Least Skewed

RankVariableSkewnessDirectionStatus
1SOD+0.892Positive — right tail⚠ Moderate
2Section 1 %+0.586Positive — right tail⚠ Moderate
3CF+0.501Positive — right tailBorderline
4Section 2 %+0.464Positive — right tailSlight
5RM+0.127Approximately symmetric✓ Acceptable
6Grand Total %+0.050Approximately symmetric✓ Acceptable
7CE−0.128Approximately symmetric✓ Acceptable

Interpretation

SOD (+0.892) — most problematic: The vast majority of candidates received a rating of exactly 3, with a thin tail extending toward 4. The Q-Q plot (W=0.576, p<.001) shows severe staircase stepping — a floor effect where assessors cluster ratings at the lower-middle of the usable scale. This restricted variance will attenuate SOD's correlations with criterion variables and reduce its predictive utility. Assessor calibration training with behavioural anchors is the primary recommended remedy.

Section 1 % (+0.586): Positive skew from a cluster of candidates scoring 48–52% with a small group reaching 86%. Despite the skew, Shapiro-Wilk does not reject normality (p=.093), keeping it within tolerable limits for parametric analysis.

Grand Total % (skew ≈ +0.050): Near-perfectly symmetric. The composite formula (Sect1 × 0.40 + Sect2 × 0.60) dampens distributional asymmetry. Normality holds (p=.172). This is the strongest variable for parametric analyses.

Normality conclusion: All competency ratings are non-normal (Shapiro-Wilk all p<.001) — expected for discrete 1–5 integer scales. Grand Total % and Section 1 % satisfy normality and can be used directly in parametric analyses. For competency scores, cross-validate with Spearman correlations and Mann-Whitney U tests.
02

Cut Score Analysis

Cut score applied: Grand Total ≥ 60.0%. Set near the group mean (M=61.25%), representing a minimum acceptable standard. This is a provisional pilot cut. Formal Angoff or contrasting groups standard-setting is required before operational use.

Output

Overall pass rate: 54.2% (13 / 24 scored candidates) Pass rate by performance group: Exceeding: 100% (3/3) Performing: 38% (3/8) ← near-identical to Off standard Off standard: 40% (4/10) ← limited discrimination Adverse Impact Ratios (4/5ths rule — flag if AI < 0.80): Gender: Male 50% AI = 1.00 (reference) Female 44% AI = 0.89 ✓ above threshold Race: White 67% AI = 1.00 (reference) Asian 57% AI = 0.86 ✓ above threshold African American 33% AI = 0.50 ⚠ below threshold — n=6, interpret with caution Mixed Race 0% AI = 0.00 ⚠ n=2 — do not interpret

Plot — Cut Score Analysis

Cut Score Sensitivity

Cut ScoreOverall PassExceeding PassPerforming PassOff Std PassVerdict
55%75%100%75%70%Too permissive
60%54%100%38%40%✓ Recommended
65%33%100%25%20%Defensible
70%21%67%13%10%Too strict

Interpretation

Critical finding — limited discrimination between Performing and Off standard: Pass rates of 38% and 40% for these groups are statistically indistinguishable. A valid cut score should show substantially higher Performing pass rates. Likely causes: range restriction in scores (47–73%), criterion contamination in line manager ratings, and small n per group (8 and 10).

African American AI ratio: The 0.50 ratio falls below the 4/5ths threshold but is based on n=6 — one additional pass raises it to 0.67. This is a monitoring flag only. Formal adverse impact analysis requires n≥30 per subgroup.

03

Classical Test Theory (CTT)

Output

Cronbach's α = 0.917 [Bootstrap 95% CI: 0.849, 0.964] McDonald's ω = 0.923 [ωh = 0.876 — general factor explains 71.3% of variance] Item-Total Correlations (corrected): RM: r = 0.897 p < .001 ✓ CF: r = 0.889 p < .001 ✓ CE: r = 0.785 p < .001 ✓ SOD: r = 0.695 p < .001 ✓ (lowest — see note) Alpha-if-Item-Deleted: RM: α = 0.862 ↓ drops — contributing positively CF: α = 0.866 ↓ drops — contributing positively CE: α = 0.901 ↓ minor drop SOD: α = 0.930 ↑ rises marginally — weakest item Inter-Item Correlations: RM CE CF SOD RM 1.000 0.762 0.909 0.711 CE 0.762 1.000 0.789 0.592 CF 0.909 0.789 1.000 0.662 SOD 0.711 0.592 0.662 1.000 ↑ RM–CF r = 0.909 — approaching redundancy threshold

Plot — CTT Internal Consistency

Item Summary

ItemCorrected rα-if-deletedΔαVerdict
RM0.8970.862−0.055✓ Excellent — retain
CF0.8890.866−0.051✓ Excellent — retain
CE0.7850.901−0.016✓ Strong — retain
SOD0.6950.930+0.013⚠ Monitor — retain for now
Overall verdict: α = 0.917 meets the "Excellent" threshold (≥0.90) for high-stakes individual assessment. The bootstrap lower bound (0.849) still exceeds the minimum 0.80 required for selection use. RM–CF inter-item r = 0.909 warrants CFA investigation at full sample.
04

Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA)

Output

Observed eigenvalues: [3.217, 0.436, 0.258, 0.089] Parallel Analysis 95th %: [1.736, 1.252, 0.995, 0.764] → Factor 1 only exceeds PA threshold → RETAIN 1 FACTOR 1-Factor PAF Loadings: RM: λ = 0.940 h² = 0.884 u² = 0.116 CF: λ = 0.938 h² = 0.881 u² = 0.119 CE: λ = 0.814 h² = 0.662 u² = 0.338 SOD: λ = 0.723 h² = 0.522 u² = 0.478 Variance explained (1 factor): 73.7% 2-Factor Varimax Loadings: RM: F1 = 0.938 F2 = 0.074 → all variance on F1 CE: F1 = 0.813 F2 = 0.031 CF: F1 = 0.938 F2 = 0.037 SOD: F1 = 0.719 F2 = 0.084 F2 variance = 0.01 — trivial; confirms unidimensional structure RMSEA (1-factor model fit): 0.000 [0.000, 0.241] BIC: −8.31 (negative = model fits well)

Plot — Scree, Factor Loadings & Loading Space

Factor Loadings Table

ItemLoading (λ)Communality (h²)Uniqueness (u²)Status
RM0.9400.8840.116✓ Excellent
CF0.9380.8810.119✓ Excellent
CE0.8140.6620.338✓ Strong
SOD0.7230.5220.478⚠ Adequate

Interpretation

Clear unidimensional structure: The first eigenvalue (3.217) is nearly twice the parallel analysis threshold (1.736). Factors 2–4 all fall below their thresholds. A single general competency factor accounts for 73.7% of total variance — this justifies treating the composite Section 2 score as a meaningful unidimensional scale.

RM–CF near-identity confirmed by EFA: RM and CF load at 0.940 and 0.938 respectively — separated by only 0.002. In the 2-factor varimax solution, when a second factor is forced, both items load entirely on F1 with F2 variance = 0.01. These two dimensions are empirically indistinguishable at the item level. The case study needs to present scenarios that genuinely differentiate "building relationships" (RM) from "focusing on client needs" (CF).

CFA recommendation: Run lavaan::cfa() in R to test the 1-factor model formally and obtain fit indices (CFI, RMSEA, SRMR). If RM–CF correlation > 0.90 replicates at full sample, consider a bifactor model or revising item content.
05

Item Response Theory (IRT)

Python note: Full parametric IRT (GRM, 2PL) requires R mirt. The Python pipeline uses logistic regression discrimination proxies for Section 2, and simulated 2PL curves for Section 1 (based on observed MCQ totals). Directionally consistent with full IRT — use R for publication-quality estimates.

Output — Section 2 Discrimination Parameters

Section 2 — Mean Discrimination (ā) per Competency: RM: ā = 2.249 ← high discrimination CF: ā = 2.278 ← high discrimination (highest) CE: ā = 2.205 ← high discrimination SOD: ā = 1.802 ← adequate (lowest — consistent with CTT) Threshold locations b (P(X≥k) = 0.50 at θ = b): RM: P(X≥4): b = 0.54 CE: P(X≥4): b = 0.21 CF: P(X≥4): b = 0.36 SOD: P(X≥3): b = 0.77 ← more stringent assessor standard for SOD

Output — Section 1 Item Parameters (2PL proxy)

Item a b Flag Q01 1.304 −2.274 easy Q02 1.498 −1.254 Q03 1.381 −0.907 Q04 1.381 −0.907 Q05 1.234 −0.766 Q06 1.012 −0.232 Q07 1.736 0.090 most discriminating Q08 0.675 1.124 ← low discrimination — flag for revision Q09 0.892 0.655 Q10 1.164 0.536 Q11 1.872 0.235 Q12 1.308 0.686 Q13 1.164 0.536 Q14 1.388 −0.048 Q15 1.033 1.566 hard

Plot — Category Response Curves & Item Discrimination

Interpretation

Section 2 — high discrimination, restricted range: All four competencies show high discrimination (ā > 1.80), meaning they reliably differentiate candidates across the latent trait continuum. However, the CRCs show only the P(X≥4) threshold is operationally useful — nearly all candidates exceed thresholds 2 and 3 automatically, confirming the floor effect. The IRT threshold analysis makes the floor effect more precise than skewness alone: rating of 3 is the effective minimum across all four competencies.

SOD threshold location (b=0.77 for P(X≥3)): A candidate needs to be 0.77 SD above the mean on the latent trait before there is a 50% chance of receiving even a rating of 3 on SOD. This is higher than CE (b=0.21 for ≥4) and CF (b=0.36 for ≥4), confirming SOD assessors apply a more conservative standard — assessor conservatism on SOD rather than poor item quality.

Section 1 — Q08 flagged: Q08 (a=0.675) falls below the minimum acceptable discrimination threshold of 0.80. A low-discriminating item provides little information — candidates at all ability levels have similar probability of answering correctly. Q08 would be flagged for revision or replacement in an operational item review. Note these are simulated items — real item-level MCQ/TF data needed for definitive conclusions.

R mirt code: grm <- mirt(comp_data, 1, itemtype="graded") — produces GRM parameters, item information curves, test information, and empirical reliability (expected ω ≈ 0.89–0.92).
06

Regression Analysis

Output

Model 1 — Grand Total only (n=21): R² = 0.142 adj-R² = 0.096 AIC = −13.4 ← best AIC grand_total: b=0.032 SE=0.018 t=1.771 p=0.093 std-β=0.376 Model 2 — Competencies only (n=21): R² = 0.314 adj-R² = 0.142 AIC = −12.1 rm: b=−0.248 SE=0.701 t=−0.354 p=0.728 std-β=−0.183 ce: b= 0.945 SE=0.404 t= 2.342 p=0.032 * std-β= 0.747 ← only sig. predictor cf: b=−0.224 SE=0.552 t=−0.406 p=0.690 std-β=−0.202 sod: b= 0.114 SE=0.607 t= 0.188 p=0.854 std-β= 0.068 Model 3 — Full model (n=21): R² = 0.318 adj-R² = 0.090 AIC = −10.3 ← worst AIC (overfitted) sect1_pct: b=0.004 SE=0.014 t=0.287 p=0.778 std-β=0.069 ce: b=0.906 SE=0.437 t=2.073 p=0.056 std-β=0.716 VIF (Model 2): rm=3.42 ce=1.98 cf=3.61 sod=1.74 ← all < 5, tolerable

Plot — Standardised Betas, Model Comparison & Predictor Scatterplots

Plot — Regression Diagnostics (Model 2)

Model Comparison

ModelR²adj-R²AICVerdict
M1 Grand Total0.1420.096−13.4Best parsimony
M2 Competencies0.3140.142−12.1✓ Recommended
M3 Full0.3180.090−10.3Overfitted

Interpretation

CE is the only significant predictor of performance (std-β=0.747, p=0.032). Across all three models, CE (Communication Effectiveness) is the strongest predictor. RM, CF, and SOD are all non-significant (p > 0.69) — despite positive bivariate correlations with performance — due to multicollinearity with CE (VIF up to 3.61).

Why sign reversal on RM and CF: RM (std-β=−0.183) and CF (std-β=−0.202) show negative betas in M2 despite positive bivariate relationships. This is a classic multicollinearity artefact: RM and CF are near-collinear with CE (from the EFA, all three load on the same factor at λ > 0.81). Their unique variance after controlling for CE is negligible and sign-reversed.

Practical implication: The current equal-weighting of all four competencies in the Section 2 composite does not reflect differential criterion validity. CE carries substantially more predictive weight. Consider differential weighting (e.g., CE × 1.5, others × 0.75) to be validated at n≥150.

Diagnostics: Residuals vs Fitted shows acceptable scatter; Q-Q plot residuals are approximately normal (Shapiro-Wilk acceptable); mild heteroscedasticity in Scale-Location plot — not severe at n=21. Model assumptions are adequately met for pilot-stage analysis.
07

Monte Carlo Simulation

Method: 500 replicates per condition · MVN from pilot empirical parameters · Seeds: 2024 (n=250), 2025 (n=500)

Output

Pilot n=27 n=250 n=500 Cronbach's α 0.917 M=0.862 CI [0.834, 0.887] M=0.861 CI [0.840, 0.880] CI width = 0.053 CI width = 0.040 (25% narrower) Pass Rate at Cut=60% 48% M=56.2% CI [50.4%, 62.0%] M=56.1% CI [51.5%, 60.4%] CI width = 11.6pp CI width = 8.9pp (23% narrower) R² (Comp → Grand Total) — CI width = 0.184 CI width = 0.121 (34% narrower)

Plot — Monte Carlo Parameter Stability

Interpretation

Alpha stabilises below pilot estimate: Simulated α (~0.862) is lower than pilot α (0.917) — expected regression to the mean, as small samples produce upward-biased estimates. The [0.840, 0.880] CI at n=500 remains solidly in the "Good" range, supporting deployment confidence.

Pass rate shifts upward: Projected true population pass rate (~56%) is higher than the pilot's 48%, likely because three candidates with missing scores are excluded from the pilot calculation. Plan selection ratios around ~56% at cut=60%.

R² needs largest samples: The 34% CI width reduction from n=250 to n=500 confirms R² is the least stable parameter. Defer formal validity claims until n≥150 operational data is available.

08

Equity & Fairness Analysis

Output

One-way ANOVA — Grand Total by Race (n=24): F(3, 20) = 2.41 p = 0.097 η² = 0.266 (large effect, underpowered) Descriptives by Race: White: n=9 M=62.3 SD=5.7 Asian: n=7 M=65.3 SD=6.7 African American: n=6 M=57.7 SD=7.0 Mixed Race: n=2 M=49.0 SD=2.8 t-test — Grand Total by Gender (n=24): t(22) = 0.89 p = 0.384 Cohen's d = 0.254 (small)

Plot — Equity Analysis by Race, Gender & Qualification

Interpretation

Race — large effect, insufficient power: ANOVA is non-significant (p=.097) but η²=0.266 is a large effect by Cohen's benchmarks. Power ≈ 0.22 at these group sizes — the test simply cannot detect real differences. The 7.6-point Asian–African American gap straddles the cut score but cannot be acted on at n<10 per group.

Gender — reassuring: d=0.254 is small, not statistically significant, CI crosses zero. Male–female score distributions overlap substantially and both straddle the cut score symmetrically.

Equity conclusion: No statistically significant adverse impact detected in the pilot. African American AI ratio (0.50) is a monitoring flag only at n=6. Re-analyse when n≥30 per subgroup.
09

Key Findings Summary

#FindingResultStatus
1Section 2 Cronbach's α0.917 [0.849, 0.964]✓ Excellent reliability
2McDonald's ω (total)0.923✓ Convergent reliability
3EFA factor structure1 factor, 73.7% variance✓ Strong unidimensionality
4Most skewed variableSOD skew = +0.892⚠ Floor effect — calibrate
5IRT — SOD discriminationā = 1.802⚠ Lowest — monitor
6RM–CF inter-item rr = 0.909⚠ Near-redundant — CFA needed
7Overall pass rate (cut=60%)54.2% (13/24)✓ Reasonable selection ratio
8Exceeding candidates pass rate100% (3/3)✓ Cut captures top performers
9Performing vs Off Std pass rates38% vs 40%⚠ Limited discrimination
10Sole significant regression predictorCE std-β = 0.747, p=0.032CE dominates — consider weighting
11M2 (Competencies → Performance) R²0.314 adj-R² = 0.142⚠ Moderate — validate at n≥150
12Gender Cohen's dd = 0.254 (small)✓ No meaningful disadvantage
13African American AI ratio0.50 (n=6)⚠ Monitor — n too small
14MC α CI at n=500[0.840, 0.880]✓ Stable across simulations
15Section 1 item Q08a = 0.675 (low)⚠ Flag for item revision

Priority Recommendations

  1. Assessor calibration for SOD — introduce behavioural anchor examples to reduce floor clustering (skew = +0.892, b-threshold = 0.77)
  2. CFA at full sample — test 1-factor vs bifactor model; investigate RM–CF near-redundancy (r=0.909, EFA loadings 0.940 vs 0.938)
  3. Differential CE weighting — CE is the sole significant predictor; current equal weighting does not reflect criterion validity
  4. Formal cut score standard-setting — Angoff or contrasting groups method before operational deployment
  5. Adverse impact re-analysis at n≥30 per subgroup — current race-group findings are statistically unreliable
  6. Item-level MCQ/TF data — enable proper 2PL IRT; revise Q08 (a=0.675)
AX Consult Group · Jurgen Becker, PhD · Business Manager Selection Assessment · Anonymised for public portfolio use
